text.NABRE: 22:22 Paul Imprisoned. They listened to him until he said this, but then they raised their voices and shouted, “Take such a one as this away from the earth. It is not right that he should live.”

text.NRSV: While they were shouting and throwing off their cloaks and tossing dust into the air,
text.Rotherham: Now, as they were both making an outcry and tearing their mantles,—dust, also were throwing into the air,
text.KJV: 22:23 And as they cried out, and cast off their clothes, & threw dust into the aire,
text.DRV: 22:23 And when they cried out, and threw off their garments, and cast dust into the air,
text.NABRE: 22:23 And as they were yelling and throwing off their cloaks and flinging dust into the air,

text.NRSV: the tribune directed that he was to be brought into the barracks, and ordered him to be examined by flogging, to find out why they were shouting against him in this way.
text.Rotherham: the captain ordered him to be brought into the castle, saying, that, with scourging, he should be put to the test,—that he might find out, for what cause they were, thus, clamouring against him.
text.KJV: 22:24 The chiefe captaine commanded him to be brought into the castle, and bade that hee should be examined by scourging: that he might know wherfore they cried so against him.
text.DRV: 22:24 the Tribune commanded him to be carried into the castle, and to be beaten with whips, & that he should be tormented: to know for what cause they did so cry at him.
text.NABRE: 22:24 the cohort commander ordered him to be brought into the compound and gave instruction that he be interrogated under the lash to determine the reason why they were making such an outcry against him.

text.NRSV: But when they had tied him up with thongs, Paul said to the centurion who was standing by, “Is it legal for you to flog a Roman citizen who is uncondemned?”
text.Rotherham: But, when they had stretched him out with straps, Paul said unto the by-standing centurion—A Roman, and uncondemned, is it allowed you to be scourging?
text.KJV: 22:25 And as they bound him with thongs, Paul said vnto the Centurion that stood by, Is it lawfull for you to scourge a man that is a Romane, and vncondemned?
text.DRV: 22:25 And when they had bound him very straight with thongs, Paul saith to the Centurion standing by him: Is it lawful for you to whip a man that is a Roman and uncondemned?
text.NABRE: 22:25 But when they had stretched him out for the whips, Paul said to the centurion on duty, “Is it lawful for you to scourge a man who is a Roman citizen and has not been tried?”

text.NRSV: When the centurion heard that, he went to the tribune and said to him, “What are you about to do? This man is a Roman citizen.”
text.Rotherham: And, when the centurion heard [that], he went unto the captain, and reported, saying—What art thou going to do? For, this man, is, a Roman?
text.KJV: 22:26 When the Centurion heard that, hee went and told the chiefe captaine, saying, Take heede what thou doest, for this man is a Romane.
text.DRV: 22:26 Which the Centurion hearing, went to the Tribune, and told him, saying: What wilt thou do? for this man is a citizen of Rome.
text.NABRE: 22:26 When the centurion heard this, he went to the cohort commander and reported it, saying, “What are you going to do? This man is a Roman citizen.”

text.NRSV: The tribune came and asked Paul, “Tell me, are you a Roman citizen?” And he said, “Yes.”
text.Rotherham: And the captain, coming up, said to him—Tell me! Art, thou, a, Roman? And, he, said—Yea!
text.KJV: 22:27 Then the chiefe captaine came; and said vnto him, Tell me, art thou a Romane? He said, Yea.
text.DRV: 22:27 And the Tribune coming, said to him: Tell me, art thou a Roman? But he said: Yea.
text.NABRE: 22:27 Then the commander came and said to him, “Tell me, are you a Roman citizen?” “Yes,” he answered.

text.NRSV: The tribune answered, “It cost me a large sum of money to get my citizenship.” Paul said, “But I was born a citizen.”
text.Rotherham: And the captain answered—I, for a large sum, this citizenship acquired! And, Paul, said—But, I, am even, [free-] born!
text.KJV: 22:28 And the chiefe captaine answered, With a great summe obteined I this freedome. And Paul said, But I was free borne.
text.DRV: 22:28 And the Tribune answered: I obtained this city with a great sum. And Paul said: But I was also born to it.
text.NABRE: 22:28 The commander replied, “I acquired this citizenship for a large sum of money.” Paul said, “But I was born one.”

text.NRSV: Immediately those who were about to examine him drew back from him; and the tribune also was afraid, for he realized that Paul was a Roman citizen and that he had bound him.
text.Rotherham: Straightway, therefore, they who were about to put him to the test, withdrew from him; and, even the captain, was struck with fear, when he found out he was, a Roman, and because, him, he had bound.
text.KJV: 22:29 Then straightway they departed from him which should haue examined him: and the chiefe captaine also was afraid after he knew that he was a Romane, & because he had bound him.
text.DRV: 22:29 Immediately therefore they departed from him that were to torment him. The Tribune also feared after he understood that he was a citizen of Rome, and because he had bound him.
text.NABRE: 22:29 At once those who were going to interrogate him backed away from him, and the commander became alarmed when he realized that he was a Roman citizen and that he had had him bound.

text.NRSV: Since he wanted to find out what Paul was being accused of by the Jews, the next day he released him and ordered the chief priests and the entire council to meet. He brought Paul down and had him stand before them.
text.Rotherham: But, on the morrow, being minded to get to know the certainty as to why he was being accused by the Jews, he released him, and ordered the High-priests and all the High-council to come together; and, bringing down Paul, set him before them.
text.KJV: 22:30 On the morrow, because he would haue knowen the certaintie wherefore he was accused of the Iewes, he loosed him from his bands, and commanded the chiefe Priests and all their Councill to appeare, and brought Paul downe, and set him before them.
text.DRV: 22:30 But the next day meaning to know more diligently for what cause he was accused of the Jews, he loosed him, and commanded the Priests to come together and all the Council: & bringing forth Paul, he set him among them.
text.NABRE: 22:30 Paul Before the Sanhedrin. The next day, wishing to determine the truth about why he was being accused by the Jews, he freed him and ordered the chief priests and the whole Sanhedrin to convene. Then he brought Paul down and made him stand before them.

text.Base: While Paul was looking intently at the council he said, “Brothers, up to this day I have lived my life with a clear conscience before God.”
text.KJV: 23:1 And Paul earnestly beholding the council, said, Men and brethren, I haue liued in all good conscience before God vntill this day.
text.DRV: 23:1 And Paul looking upon the Council, said: Men brethren, I with all good conscience have conversed before God, until this present day.
text.NABRE: 23:1 Chapter 23 - Paul looked intently at the Sanhedrin and said, “My brothers, I have conducted myself with a perfectly clear conscience before God to this day.”

text.Base: Then the high priest Ananias ordered those standing near him to strike him on the mouth.
text.KJV: 23:2 And the high Priest Ananias commanded them that stood by him, to smite him on the mouth.
text.DRV: 23:2 And the high Priest Ananias commanded them that stood by him, to smite him on the mouth.
text.NABRE: 23:2 The high priest Ananias ordered his attendants to strike his mouth.

text.Base: At this Paul said to him, “God will strike you, you whitewashed wall! Are you sitting there to judge me according to the law, and yet in violation of the law you order me to be struck?”
text.KJV: 23:3 Then saith Paul vnto him, God shall smite thee, thou whited wall: for sittest thou to iudge mee after the Law, and commandest mee to be smitten contrary to the Law?
text.DRV: 23:3 Then Paul said to him: God shall strike thee, thou whited wall. And thou sitting judgest me according to the law, and contrary to law doest thou command me to be smitten?
text.NABRE: 23:3 Then Paul said to him, “God will strike you, you whitewashed wall. Do you indeed sit in judgment upon me according to the law and yet in violation of the law order me to be struck?”

text.Base: Those standing nearby said, “Do you dare to insult God’s high priest?”
text.KJV: 23:4 And they that stood by, said, Reuilest thou Gods high Priest?
text.DRV: 23:4 And they that stood by, said: Doest thou revile the high Priest of God?
text.NABRE: 23:4 The attendants said, “Would you revile God’s high priest?”

text.Base: And Paul said, “I did not realize, brothers, that he was high priest; for it is written, ‘You shall not speak evil of a leader of your people.’ ”
text.KJV: 23:5 Then said Paul, I wist not, brethren, that hee was the high Prist: For it is written, Thou shalt not speake euill of the ruler of thy people.
text.DRV: 23:5 And Paul said: I knew not, brethren, that he is the high Priest. For it is written: The prince of thy people thou shalt not misspeak.
text.NABRE: 23:5 Paul answered, “Brothers, I did not realize he was the high priest. For it is written, ‘You shall not curse a ruler of your people.’”

text.Base: When Paul noticed that some were Sadducees and others were Pharisees, he called out in the council, “Brothers, I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees. I am on trial concerning the hope of the resurrection of the dead.”
text.KJV: 23:6 But when Paul perceiued that the one part were Sadducees, and the other Pharisees, hee cryed out in the Councill, Men and brethren, I am a Pharisee, the sonne of a Pharisee: of the hope and resurrection of the dead, I am called in question.
text.DRV: 23:6 And Paul knowing that the one part was of Sadducees, and the other of Pharisees, he cried out in the Council: Men brethren, I am a Pharisee, the son of Pharisees: of the hope and resurrection of the dead am I judged.
text.NABRE: 23:6 Paul was aware that some were Sadducees and some Pharisees, so he called out before the Sanhedrin, “My brothers, I am a Pharisee, the son of Pharisees; [I] am on trial for hope in the resurrection of the dead.”

text.Base: When he said this, a dissension began between the Pharisees and the Sadducees, and the assembly was divided.
text.KJV: 23:7 And when hee had so said, there arose a dissension betweene the Pharisees and the Sadducees: and the multitude was diuided.
text.DRV: 23:7 And when he had said these things, there rose dissension between the Pharisees and Sadducees; and the multitude was divided.
text.NABRE: 23:7 When he said this, a dispute broke out between the Pharisees and Sadducees, and the group became divided.

text.Base: (The Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, or angel, or spirit; but the Pharisees acknowledge all three.)
text.KJV: 23:8 For the Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, neither Angel, nor spirit: but the Pharisees confesse both.
text.DRV: 23:8 For the Sadducees say there is no resurrection, nor Angel, nor spirit: but the Pharisees confess both.
text.NABRE: 23:8 For the Sadducees say that there is no resurrection or angels or spirits, while the Pharisees acknowledge all three.

text.Base: Then a great clamor arose, and certain scribes of the Pharisees’ group stood up and contended, “We find nothing wrong with this man. What if a spirit or an angel has spoken to him?”
text.KJV: 23:9 And there arose a great cry: and the Scribes that were of the Pharisees part arose, and stroue, saying, Wee finde no euill in this man: but if a spirit or an Angel hath spoken to him, let vs not fight against God.
text.DRV: 23:9 And there was made a great cry. And certain of the Pharisees rising up, strove saying: We find no evil in this man. What if a spirit hath spoken to him, or an Angel?
text.NABRE: 23:9 A great uproar occurred, and some scribes belonging to the Pharisee party stood up and sharply argued, “We find nothing wrong with this man. Suppose a spirit or an angel has spoken to him?”

text.Base: When the dissension became violent, the tribune, fearing that they would tear Paul to pieces, ordered the soldiers to go down, take him by force, and bring him into the barracks.
text.KJV: 23:10 And when there arose a great dissension, the chiefe captaine fearing lest Paul should haue bene pulled in pieces of them, commanded the souldiers to goe downe, and to take him by force from among them, and to bring him into the castle.
text.DRV: 23:10 And when there was risen great dissension, the Tribune fearing lest Paul should be torn in pieces by them, commanded the soldiers to go down, and to take him out of the midst of them, and to bring him into the castle.
text.NABRE: 23:10 The dispute was so serious that the commander, afraid that Paul would be torn to pieces by them, ordered his troops to go down and rescue him from their midst and take him into the compound.

text.Base: That night the Lord stood by him and said, “Keep up your courage! For just as you have testified for me in Jerusalem, so you must bear witness also in Rome.”
text.KJV: 23:11 And the night folowing, the Lord stood by him, and saide, Bee of good cheere, Paul: for as thou hast testified of mee in Hierusalem, so must thou beare witnesse also at Rome.
text.DRV: 23:11 And the night following our Lord standing by him, said: Be constant; for as thou hast testified of me in Jerusalem, so must thou testify at Rome also.
text.NABRE: 23:11 The following night the Lord stood by him and said, “Take courage. For just as you have borne witness to my cause in Jerusalem, so you must also bear witness in Rome.”

text.Base: In the morning the Jews joined in a conspiracy and bound themselves by an oath neither to eat nor drink until they had killed Paul.
text.KJV: 23:12 And when it was day, certaine of the Iewes banded together, and bound themselues vnder a curse, saying, that they would neither eate nor drinke till they had killed Paul.
text.DRV: 23:12 And when day was come, certain of the Jews gathered themselves together, & vowed themselves, saying: that they would neither eat nor drink till they killed Paul.
text.NABRE: 23:12 Transfer to Caesarea. When day came, the Jews made a plot and bound themselves by oath not to eat or drink until they had killed Paul.

text.Base: There were more than forty who joined in this conspiracy.
text.KJV: 23:13 And they were more then fourtie which had made this conspiracie.
text.DRV: 23:13 And they were more than forty men that had made this conspiracy:
text.NABRE: 23:13 There were more than forty who formed this conspiracy.

text.Base: They went to the chief priests and elders and said, “We have strictly bound ourselves by an oath to taste no food until we have killed Paul.
text.KJV: 23:14 And they came to the chiefe Priests and Elders, and said, Wee haue bound our selues vnder a great curse, that wee will eate nothing vntill wee haue slaine Paul.
text.DRV: 23:14 who came to the chief priests and the ancients, and said: By execration we have vowed ourselves, that we will eat nothing, till we kill Paul.
text.NABRE: 23:14 They went to the chief priests and elders and said, “We have bound ourselves by a solemn oath to taste nothing until we have killed Paul.

text.Base: Now then, you and the council must notify the tribune to bring him down to you, on the pretext that you want to make a more thorough examination of his case. And we are ready to do away with him before he arrives.”
text.KJV: 23:15 Now therefore ye with the Councill, signifie to the chiefe captaine that he bring him downe vnto you to morrow, as though yee would enquire something more perfectly concerning him: and we, or euer he come neere, are ready to kill him.
text.DRV: 23:15 Now therefore give you knowledge to the Tribune with the Council, that he bring him forth to you, as if you meant to know some more certainty touching him. But we, before he come near, are ready for to kill him.
text.NABRE: 23:15 You, together with the Sanhedrin, must now make an official request to the commander to have him bring him down to you, as though you meant to investigate his case more thoroughly. We on our part are prepared to kill him before he arrives.”

text.Base: Now the son of Paul’s sister heard about the ambush; so he went and gained entrance to the barracks and told Paul.
text.KJV: 23:16 And when Pauls sisters sonne heard of their laying in wait, hee went and entred into the castle, & told Paul.
text.DRV: 23:16 Which when Paul's sister's son had heard, of their lying in wait, he came and entered into the castle and told Paul.
text.NABRE: 23:16 The son of Paul’s sister, however, heard about the ambush; so he went and entered the compound and reported it to Paul.

text.Base: Paul called one of the centurions and said, “Take this young man to the tribune, for he has something to report to him.”
text.KJV: 23:17 Then Paul called one of the Centurions vnto him, and said, Bring this yong man vnto the chiefe captaine: for he hath a certaine thing to tell him.
text.DRV: 23:17 And Paul calling to him one of the Centurions, said: Bring this young man to the Tribune, for he hath something to tell him.
text.NABRE: 23:17 Paul then called one of the centurions and requested, “Take this young man to the commander; he has something to report to him.”

text.Base: So he took him, brought him to the tribune, and said, “The prisoner Paul called me and asked me to bring this young man to you; he has something to tell you.”
text.KJV: 23:18 So he took him, and brought him to the chiefe captaine, and said, Paul the prisoner called me vnto him, and praied mee to bring this yong man vnto thee, who hath something to say vnto thee.
text.DRV: 23:18 And he taking him, brought him to the Tribune, and said: The prisoner Paul desired me to bring this young man unto thee, having something to say to thee.
text.NABRE: 23:18 So he took him and brought him to the commander and explained, “The prisoner Paul called me and asked that I bring this young man to you; he has something to say to you.”

text.Base: The tribune took him by the hand, drew him aside privately, and asked, “What is it that you have to report to me?”
text.KJV: 23:19 Then the chiefe captaine tooke him by the hand, and went with him aside priuately, and asked him, What is that thou hast to tell me?
text.DRV: 23:19 And the Tribune taking him by the hand, went aside with him apart, and asked him: What is it that thou hast to tell me?
text.NABRE: 23:19 The commander took him by the hand, drew him aside, and asked him privately, “What is it you have to report to me?”

text.Base: He answered, “The Jews have agreed to ask you to bring Paul down to the council tomorrow, as though they were going to inquire more thoroughly into his case.
text.KJV: 23:20 And he said, The Iewes haue agreed to desire thee, that thou wouldest bring downe Paul to morrow into the Council, as though they would enquire somewhat of him more perfectly.
text.DRV: 23:20 And he said: The Jews have agreed to desire thee, that tomorrow thou wilt bring forth Paul into the Council, as though they meant to inquire some more certainty touching him.
text.NABRE: 23:20 He replied, “The Jews have conspired to ask you to bring Paul down to the Sanhedrin tomorrow, as though they meant to inquire about him more thoroughly,

text.Base: But do not be persuaded by them, for more than forty of their men are lying in ambush for him. They have bound themselves by an oath neither to eat nor drink until they have killed him. They are ready now and are waiting for your consent.”
text.KJV: 23:21 But do not thou yeeld vnto them: for there lie in wait for him of them moe then fourtie men, which haue bound themselues with an othe, that they will neither eate nor drinke, till they haue killed him: and now are they ready, looking for a promise from thee.
text.DRV: 23:21 But do not thou credit them; for there lie in wait for him more than forty men of them, which have vowed neither to eat nor to drink, till they kill him: and they are now ready, expecting thy promise.
text.NABRE: 23:21 but do not believe them. More than forty of them are lying in wait for him; they have bound themselves by oath not to eat or drink until they have killed him. They are now ready and only wait for your consent.”

text.Base: So the tribune dismissed the young man, ordering him, “Tell no one that you have informed me of this.”
text.KJV: 23:22 So the chiefe captaine then let the yong man depart, and charged him, See thou tell no man, that thou hast shewed these things to me.
text.DRV: 23:22 The Tribune therefore dismissed the young man, commanding that he should speak to no man that he had notified these things unto him.
text.NABRE: 23:22 As the commander dismissed the young man he directed him, “Tell no one that you gave me this information.”

text.Base: Then he summoned two of the centurions and said, “Get ready to leave for Caesarea by nine o’clock tonight with two hundred soldiers, seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen.
text.KJV: 23:23 And he called vnto him two Centurions, saying, Make ready two hundred souldiers to goe to Cesarea, and horsemen threescore and ten, and spearemen two hundred, at the third houre of the night.
text.DRV: 23:23 And calling two Centurions, he said to them: Make ready two hundred soldiers, to go as far as Cæsarea, and seventy horsemen, and lances two hundred, from the third hour of the night:
text.NABRE: 23:23 Then he summoned two of the centurions and said, “Get two hundred soldiers ready to go to Caesarea by nine o’clock tonight, along with seventy horsemen and two hundred auxiliaries.

text.Base: Also provide mounts for Paul to ride, and take him safely to Felix the governor.”
text.KJV: 23:24 And prouide them beasts, that they may set Paul on, and bring him safe vnto Felix the gouernour.
text.DRV: 23:24 and prepare beasts: that setting Paul on, they might bring him safe to Felix the President.
text.NABRE: 23:24 Provide mounts for Paul to ride and give him safe conduct to Felix the governor.”

text.Base: He wrote a letter to this effect: “Claudius Lysias to his Excellency the governor Felix, greetings.
text.KJV: 23:25 And hee wrote a letter after this manner:
text.DRV: 23:25 (For he feared lest perhaps the Jews might take him away, and kill him, and himself afterward should sustain reproach, as though he would have taken money)
text.NABRE: 23:25 Then he wrote a letter with this content:

text.Base: This man was seized by the Jews and was about to be killed by them, but when I had learned that he was a Roman citizen, I came with the guard and rescued him.
text.KJV: 23:27 This man was taken of the Iewes and should haue beene killed of them: Then came I with an armie, and rescued him, hauing vnderstood that he was a Romane.
text.DRV: 23:27 This man being apprehended of the Jews, and ready to be killed of them, I coming in with the band delivered him, understanding that he is a Roman:
text.NABRE: 23:27 This man, seized by the Jews and about to be murdered by them, I rescued after intervening with my troops when I learned that he was a Roman citizen.

text.Base: Wishing to know the charge for which they accused him, I had him brought to their council.
text.KJV: 23:28 And when I would haue knowen the cause wherefore they accused him, I brought him foorth into their Council.
text.DRV: 23:28 and meaning to know the cause that they objected unto him, I brought him down into their Council.
text.NABRE: 23:28 I wanted to learn the reason for their accusations against him so I brought him down to their Sanhedrin.

text.Base: I found that he was accused concerning questions of their law, but was charged with nothing deserving death or imprisonment.
text.KJV: 23:29 Whom I perceiued to be accused of questions of their lawe, but to haue nothing laide to his charge worthy of death or of bonds.
text.DRV: 23:29 Whom I found to be accused concerning questions of their law: but having no crime worthy of death or of bands.
text.NABRE: 23:29 I discovered that he was accused in matters of controversial questions of their law and not of any charge deserving death or imprisonment.

text.Base: When I was informed that there would be a plot against the man, I sent him to you at once, ordering his accusers also to state before you what they have against him.”
text.KJV: 23:30 And when it was tolde me, how that the Iewes laid waite for the man, I sent straightway to thee, and gaue commandement to his accusers also, to say before thee what they had against him. Farewell.
text.DRV: 23:30 And when it was told me of ambushments that they had prepared against him, I sent him to thee, signifying also to the accusers, to speak before thee. Fare-well.
text.NABRE: 23:30 Since it was brought to my attention that there will be a plot against the man, I am sending him to you at once, and have also notified his accusers to state [their case] against him before you.”

text.Base: So the soldiers, according to their instructions, took Paul and brought him during the night to Antipatris.
text.KJV: 23:31 Then the souldiers, as it was commaunded them, tooke Paul, and brought him by night to Antipatris.
text.DRV: 23:31 And the soldiers according as it was commanded them, taking Paul, brought him by night to Antipatris.
text.NABRE: 23:31 So the soldiers, according to their orders, took Paul and escorted him by night to Antipatris.

text.Base: The next day they let the horsemen go on with him, while they returned to the barracks.
text.KJV: 23:32 On the morow, they left the horsemen to goe with him, and returned to the castle.
text.DRV: 23:32 And the next day sending away the horse-men to go with him, they returned to the castle.
text.NABRE: 23:32 The next day they returned to the compound, leaving the horsemen to complete the journey with him.

text.Base: When they came to Caesarea and delivered the letter to the governor, they presented Paul also before him.
text.KJV: 23:33 Who when they came to Cesarea, and deliuered the Epistle to the gouernour, presented Paul also before him.
text.DRV: 23:33 Who when they were come to Cæsarea, and had delivered the letter to the President, they did set Paul also before him.
text.NABRE: 23:33 When they arrived in Caesarea they delivered the letter to the governor and presented Paul to him.

text.Base: On reading the letter, he asked what province he belonged to, and when he learned that he was from Cilicia,
text.KJV: 23:34 And when the gouernour had read the letter, he asked of what prouince he was. And when he vnderstood that he was of Cilicia:
text.DRV: 23:34 And when he had read, and had asked of what province he was: and understanding that of Cilicia:
text.NABRE: 23:34 When he had read it and asked to what province he belonged, and learned that he was from Cilicia,

text.Base: he said, “I will give you a hearing when your accusers arrive.” Then he ordered that he be kept under guard in Herod’s headquarters.
text.KJV: 23:35 I will heare thee, said hee, when thine accusers are also come. And hee commanded him to be kept in Herods iudgement hall.
text.DRV: 23:35 I will hear thee, said he, when thy accusers are come. And he commanded him to be kept in Herod's palace.
text.NABRE: 23:35 he said, “I shall hear your case when your accusers arrive.” Then he ordered that he be held in custody in Herod’s praetorium.

text.Base: Five days later the high priest Ananias came down with some elders and an attorney, a certain Tertullus, and they reported their case against Paul to the governor.
text.KJV: 24:1 And after fiue dayes, Ananias the hie Priest descended with the Elders, and with a certaine Oratour named Tertullus, who enformed the gouernour against Paul.
text.DRV: 24:1 And after five days the high priest Ananias descended, with certain ancients and one Tertullus an orator, who went to the President against Paul.
text.NABRE: 24:1 Chapter 24 - Trial Before Felix. Five days later the high priest Ananias came down with some elders and an advocate, a certain Tertullus, and they presented formal charges against Paul to the governor.

text.Base: When Paul had been summoned, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying: “Your Excellency, because of you we have long enjoyed peace, and reforms have been made for this people because of your foresight.
text.KJV: 24:2 And when he was called foorth, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying, Seeing that by thee we enioy great quietnesse, and that very worthy deeds are done vnto this natiō by thy prouidence:
text.DRV: 24:2 And Paul being cited, Tertullus began to accuse, saying: Whereas we live in much peace by thee, & many things are corrected by thy providence,
text.NABRE: 24:2 When he was called, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying, “Since we have attained much peace through you, and reforms have been accomplished in this nation through your provident care,

text.Base: In every way and everywhere we welcome this with utmost gratitude.
text.KJV: 24:3 Wee accept it alwayes, and in all places, most noble Felix, with all thankfulnesse.
text.DRV: 24:3 we do always & in all places receive it, most excellent Felix, with all thanks-giving.
text.NABRE: 24:3 we acknowledge this in every way and everywhere, most excellent Felix, with all gratitude.

text.Base: But, to detain you no further, I beg you to hear us briefly with your customary forbearance.
text.KJV: 24:4 Notwithstanding, that I be not farther tedious vnto thee, I pray thee, that thou wouldest heare vs of thy clemencie a few words.
text.DRV: 24:4 But lest I hinder thee any longer, I desire thee of thy clemency briefly to hear us.
text.NABRE: 24:4 But in order not to detain you further, I ask you to give us a brief hearing with your customary graciousness.

text.Base: We have, in fact, found this man a pestilent fellow, an agitator among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazoreans.
text.KJV: 24:5 For we haue found this man a pestilent fellow, and a moouer of sedition among all the Iewes throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes.
text.DRV: 24:5 We have found this man pestiferous, & raising seditions to all the Jews in the whole world, and author of the sedition of the sect of the Nazarenes.
text.NABRE: 24:5 We found this man to be a pest; he creates dissension among Jews all over the world and is a ringleader of the sect of the Nazoreans.

text.Base: He even tried to profane the temple, and so we seized him.
text.KJV: 24:6 Who also hath gone about to profane the Temple: whom we tooke, and would haue iudged according to our lawe.
text.DRV: 24:6 Who also hath attempted to violate the temple, whom also being apprehended we would have judged according to our law.
text.NABRE: 24:6 He even tried to desecrate our temple, but we arrested him.

text.Base: By examining him yourself you will be able to learn from him about everything of which we accuse him.”
text.KJV: 24:8 Commanding his accusers to come vnto thee, by examining of whom thy selfe mayest take knowledge of all these things, whereof we accuse him.
text.DRV: 24:8 commanding his accusers to come to thee, of whom thou mayest thyself judging, understand of all these things, whereof we accuse him.
text.NABRE: 24:8 If you examine him you will be able to learn from him for yourself about everything of which we are accusing him.”

text.Base: The Jews also joined in the charge by asserting that all this was true.
text.KJV: 24:9 And the Iewes also assented, saying that these things were so.
text.DRV: 24:9 And the Jews also added, saying that these things were so.
text.NABRE: 24:9 The Jews also joined in the attack and asserted that these things were so.

text.Base: When the governor motioned to him to speak, Paul replied: “I cheerfully make my defense, knowing that for many years you have been a judge over this nation.
text.KJV: 24:10 Then Paul, after that the gouernour had beckened vnto him to speake, answered, Forasmuch as I know that thou hast been of many yeeres a Iudge vnto this nation, I do the more cheerefully answere for my selfe:
text.DRV: 24:10 But Paul answered: (the President making a sign unto him for to speak) Knowing that of many years thou art judge over this nation, I will with good courage answer for myself.
text.NABRE: 24:10 Then the governor motioned to him to speak and Paul replied, “I know that you have been a judge over this nation for many years and so I am pleased to make my defense before you.

text.Base: As you can find out, it is not more than twelve days since I went up to worship in Jerusalem.
text.KJV: 24:11 Because that thou mayest vnderstand, that there are yet but twelue dayes, since I went vp to Hierusalem for to worship.
text.DRV: 24:11 For thou mayest understand that it is not above twelve days to me, since I went up to adore in Jerusalem.
text.NABRE: 24:11 As you can verify, not more than twelve days have passed since I went up to Jerusalem to worship.

text.Base: They did not find me disputing with anyone in the temple or stirring up a crowd either in the synagogues or throughout the city.
text.KJV: 24:12 And they neither found me in the Temple disputing with any man, neither raising vp the people, neither in the Synagogues, nor in the citie:
text.DRV: 24:12 And neither in the temple did they find me disputing with any man, or causing concourse of the multitude, neither in the synagogues, nor in the city:
text.NABRE: 24:12 Neither in the temple, nor in the synagogues, nor anywhere in the city did they find me arguing with anyone or instigating a riot among the people.

text.Base: Neither can they prove to you the charge that they now bring against me.
text.KJV: 24:13 Neither can they proue the things whereof they now accuse me.
text.DRV: 24:13 neither can they prove unto thee the things whereof they now accuse me.
text.NABRE: 24:13 Nor can they prove to you the accusations they are now making against me.

text.Base: But this I admit to you, that according to the Way, which they call a sect, I worship the God of our ancestors, believing everything laid down according to the law or written in the prophets.
text.KJV: 24:14 But this I confesse vnto thee, that after the way which they call heresie, so worship I the God of my fathers, beleeuing all things which are written in the Law and the Prophets,
text.DRV: 24:14 But this I confess to thee, that according to the sect, which they call heresy, I do so serve the Father my God, believing all things that are written in the Law & the Prophets:
text.NABRE: 24:14 But this I do admit to you, that according to the Way, which they call a sect, I worship the God of our ancestors and I believe everything that is in accordance with the law and written in the prophets.

text.Base: I have a hope in God—a hope that they themselves also accept—that there will be a resurrection of both the righteous and the unrighteous.
text.KJV: 24:15 And haue hope towards God, which they themselues also allow, that there shall be a resurrection of the dead, both of the iust and vniust.
text.DRV: 24:15 having hope in God, the which these also themselves expect, that there shall be a resurrection of just and unjust.
text.NABRE: 24:15 I have the same hope in God as they themselves have that there will be a resurrection of the righteous and the unrighteous.

text.Base: Therefore I do my best always to have a clear conscience before God and before people.
text.KJV: 24:16 And herein doe I exercise my selfe to haue alwayes a conscience void of offence toward God, and toward men.
text.DRV: 24:16 In this myself also do study to have a conscience without offence toward God and toward men always.
text.NABRE: 24:16 Because of this, I always strive to keep my conscience clear before God and man.

text.Base: Now after some years I came to bring alms to my nation and to offer sacrifices.
text.KJV: 24:17 Now after many yeeres, I came to bring almes to my nation, & offrings:
text.DRV: 24:17 And after many years I came to bestow alms upon my nation, & oblations, and vows.
text.NABRE: 24:17 After many years, I came to bring alms for my nation and offerings.

text.Base: While I was doing this, they found me in the temple, purified, with no crowd or disturbance.
text.KJV: 24:18 Wherupon certaine Iewes from Asia found me purified in the Temple, neither with multitude, nor with tumult:
text.DRV: 24:18 In the which they found me purified in the temple: not with multitude nor with tumult.
text.NABRE: 24:18 While I was so engaged, they found me, after my purification, in the temple without a crowd or disturbance.

text.Base: But there were some Jews from Asia—they ought to be here before you to make an accusation, if they have anything against me.
text.KJV: 24:19 Who ought to haue beene here before thee, and obiect, if they had ought against me.
text.DRV: 24:19 But certain Jews of Asia, who ought to be present before thee and to accuse, if they had any thing against me:
text.NABRE: 24:19 But some Jews from the province of Asia, who should be here before you to make whatever accusation they might have against me—

text.Base: Or let these men here tell what crime they had found when I stood before the council,
text.KJV: 24:20 Or else let these same here say, if they haue found any euill doing in mee, while I stood before the Councill,
text.DRV: 24:20 or let these men themselves say, if they have found in me any iniquity, forasmuch as I stand in the Council,
text.NABRE: 24:20 or let these men themselves state what crime they discovered when I stood before the Sanhedrin,

text.Base: unless it was this one sentence that I shouted while standing among them, ‘It is about the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial before you today.’ ”
text.KJV: 24:21 Except it be for this one voice, that I cried standing among them, Touching the resurrection of the dead I am called in question by you this day.
text.DRV: 24:21 but of this one voice only that I cried standing among them, That of the resurrection of the dead am I judged this day of you.
text.NABRE: 24:21 unless it was my one outcry as I stood among them, that ‘I am on trial before you today for the resurrection of the dead.’”

text.Base: But Felix, who was rather well informed about the Way, adjourned the hearing with the comment, “When Lysias the tribune comes down, I will decide your case.”
text.KJV: 24:22 And when Felix heard these things, hauing more perfect knowledge of that way, he deferred them and said, When Lysias the chiefe captaine shall come downe, I will know the vttermost of your matter.
text.DRV: 24:22 And Felix deferred them, knowing most certainly of this way, saying: When Lysias the Tribune is come down, I will hear you.
text.NABRE: 24:22 Then Felix, who was accurately informed about the Way, postponed the trial, saying, “When Lysias the commander comes down, I shall decide your case.”

text.Base: Then he ordered the centurion to keep him in custody, but to let him have some liberty and not to prevent any of his friends from taking care of his needs.
text.KJV: 24:23 And he commanded a Centurion to keepe Paul, and to let him haue libertie, and that he should forbid none of his acquaintance to minister, or come vnto him.
text.DRV: 24:23 And he commanded the Centurion to keep him, and that he should have rest, neither to prohibit any of his to minister unto him.
text.NABRE: 24:23 He gave orders to the centurion that he should be kept in custody but have some liberty, and that he should not prevent any of his friends from caring for his needs.

text.Base: Some days later when Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish, he sent for Paul and heard him speak about faith in Christ Jesus.
text.KJV: 24:24 And after certaine dayes, when Felix came with his wife Orusilla, which was a Iew, he sent for Paul, and heard him cōcerning the faith in Christ.
text.DRV: 24:24 And after some days, Felix coming with Drusilla his wife, which was a Jew, called Paul, and heard of him the faith that is in Christ Jesus.
text.NABRE: 24:24 Captivity in Caesarea. Several days later Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish. He had Paul summoned and listened to him speak about faith in Christ Jesus.

text.Base: And as he discussed justice, self-control, and the coming judgment, Felix became frightened and said, “Go away for the present; when I have an opportunity, I will send for you.”
text.KJV: 24:25 And as he reasoned of righteousnesse, temperance, and iudgement to come, Felix trembled and answered, Go thy way for this time, when I haue a conuenient season, I will call for thee.
text.DRV: 24:25 And he disputing of justice and chastity, and of the judgement to come, Felix being terrified, answered: For this time, go thy way: but in time convenient I will send for thee.
text.NABRE: 24:25 But as he spoke about righteousness and self-restraint and the coming judgment, Felix became frightened and said, “You may go for now; when I find an opportunity I shall summon you again.”

text.Base: At the same time he hoped that money would be given him by Paul, and for that reason he used to send for him very often and converse with him.
text.KJV: 24:26 He hoped also that money should haue bene giuen him of Paul, that hee might loose him: wherefore hee sent for him the oftner, and cōmuned with him.
text.DRV: 24:26 hoping also withal, that money would be given him of Paul; for the which cause also oftentimes sending for him, he spake with him.
text.NABRE: 24:26 At the same time he hoped that a bribe would be offered him by Paul, and so he sent for him very often and conversed with him.

text.Base: After two years had passed, Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus; and since he wanted to grant the Jews a favor, Felix left Paul in prison.
text.KJV: 24:27 But after two yeeres, Portius Festus came into Felix roome: and Felix willing to shew the Iewes a pleasure, left Paul bound.
text.DRV: 24:27 But when two years were ended, Felix had a Successor Portius Festus. And Felix being willing to shew the Jews a pleasure, left Paul in prison.
text.NABRE: 24:27 Two years passed and Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus. Wishing to ingratiate himself with the Jews, Felix left Paul in prison.

text.NRSV: Three days after Festus had arrived in the province, he went up from Caesarea to Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: Festus, therefore, having come upon the province, after three days, went up unto Jerusalem from Caesarea;
text.KJV: 25:1 Nowe when Festus was come into the prouince, after three dayes he ascended frō Cesarea to Hierusalem.
text.DRV: 25:1 Festus therefore when he was come into the province, after three days went up to Jerusalem from Cæsarea.
text.NABRE: 25:1 Chapter 25 - Appeal to Caesar. Three days after his arrival in the province, Festus went up from Caesarea to Jerusalem

text.NRSV: The chief priests and the leaders of the Jews reported their case against Paul, and they appealed to him
text.Rotherham: and the High-priest and chiefs of the Jews laid information before him against Paul, and began to beseech him
text.KJV: 25:2 Then the high Priest, and the chiefe of the Iewes informed him against Paul, and besought him,
text.DRV: 25:2 And the chief Priests, and principal men of the Jews went unto him against Paul: and they desired him,
text.NABRE: 25:2 where the chief priests and Jewish leaders presented him their formal charges against Paul. They asked him

text.NRSV: and requested, as a favor to them against Paul, to have him transferred to Jerusalem, for they were planning an ambush to kill him on the way.
text.Rotherham: asking for themselves as a favour against him, that he would send for him unto Jerusalem,—making, an ambush, to kill him on the way.
text.KJV: 25:3 And desired fauour against him, that he would send for him to Hierusalem, laying wait in the way to kill him.
text.DRV: 25:3 requesting favour against him, that he would command him to be brought to Jerusalem, laying wait for to kill him in the way.
text.NABRE: 25:3 as a favor to have him sent to Jerusalem, for they were plotting to kill him along the way.

text.NRSV: Festus replied that Paul was being kept at Caesarea, and that he himself intended to go there shortly.
text.Rotherham: Festus, therefore, answered, that Paul should be kept in Caesarea, and that, he himself, was about, shortly, to be going out [thither] .
text.KJV: 25:4 But Festus answered, that Paul should be kept at Cesarea, and that hee himselfe would depart shortly thither.
text.DRV: 25:4 But Festus answered, that Paul is kept in Cæsarea: and that he would very shortly go thither.
text.NABRE: 25:4 Festus replied that Paul was being held in custody in Caesarea and that he himself would be returning there shortly.

text.NRSV: “So,” he said, “let those of you who have the authority come down with me, and if there is anything wrong about the man, let them accuse him.”
text.Rotherham: They, therefore, among you (saith he) who are in power, let them go down with me; and, if there is in the man, anything amiss, let them accuse him.
text.KJV: 25:5 Let them therefore, said he, which among you are able, go downe with me, and accuse this man, if there be any wickednesse in him.
text.DRV: 25:5 They therefore, saith he, that are of ability among you, going down with me, if there be any crime in the man, let them accuse him.
text.NABRE: 25:5 He said, “Let your authorities come down with me, and if this man has done something improper, let them accuse him.”

text.NRSV: After he had stayed among them not more than eight or ten days, he went down to Caesarea; the next day he took his seat on the tribunal and ordered Paul to be brought.
text.Rotherham: And, spending among them, not more than eight or ten days, he went down unto Caesarea; and, on the morrow, taking his place upon the judgment-seat, ordered Paul to be brought.
text.KJV: 25:6 And when hee had taried among them more then ten dayes, hee went downe vnto Cesarea, and the next day sitting in the iudgement seat, commanded Paul to be brought.
text.DRV: 25:6 And having tarried among them not above eight or ten days, he went down to Cæsarea, and the next day he sat in the judgement seat: and he commanded Paul to be brought.
text.NABRE: 25:6 After spending no more than eight or ten days with them, he went down to Caesarea, and on the following day took his seat on the tribunal and ordered that Paul be brought in.

text.NRSV: When he arrived, the Jews who had gone down from Jerusalem stood around him, bringing many serious charges against him, which they could not prove.
text.Rotherham: And, when he presented himself, the Jews who, from Jerusalem, had come down, stood round about him, many and grievous charges, bringing against [him], which they were not able to prove,—
text.KJV: 25:7 And when hee was come, the Iewes which came downe from Hierusalem, stood round about, and laide many and grieuous complaints against Paul, which they could not proue,
text.DRV: 25:7 Who being brought, there stood about him the Jews that were come down from Jerusalem, objecting many and grievous causes which they could not prove;
text.NABRE: 25:7 When he appeared, the Jews who had come down from Jerusalem surrounded him and brought many serious charges against him, which they were unable to prove.

text.NRSV: Paul said in his defense, “I have in no way committed an offense against the law of the Jews, or against the temple, or against the emperor.”
text.Rotherham: Paul saying in defence—Neither against the law of the Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Caesar, have I in anything sinned.
text.KJV: 25:8 While hee answered for himselfe, Neither against the law of the Iewes, neither against the Temple, nor yet against Cesar, haue I offended any thing at all.
text.DRV: 25:8 Paul making answer, That neither against the law of the Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Cæsar have I any thing offended.
text.NABRE: 25:8 In defending himself Paul said, “I have committed no crime either against the Jewish law or against the temple or against Caesar.”

text.NRSV: But Festus, wishing to do the Jews a favor, said to Paul, “Do you wish to go up to Jerusalem and there be tried on these charges before me?”
text.Rotherham: But, Festus, wishing, with the Jews, to gain, favour, answered Paul, and said—Art thou willing, unto Jerusalem, to go up, and, there, concerning these things, be judged before me?
text.KJV: 25:9 But Festus willing to doe the Iewes a pleasure, answered Paul, and said, Wilt thou goe vp to Hierusalem, and there be iudged of these things before me?
text.DRV: 25:9 But Festus willing to shew the Jews a pleasure, answering Paul, said: Wilt thou go up to Jerusalem, and there be judged of these things before me?
text.NABRE: 25:9 Then Festus, wishing to ingratiate himself with the Jews, said to Paul in reply, “Are you willing to go up to Jerusalem and there stand trial before me on these charges?”

text.NRSV: Paul said, “I am appealing to the emperor’s tribunal; this is where I should be tried. I have done no wrong to the Jews, as you very well know.
text.Rotherham: But Paul said—Standing before the judgment-seat of Caesar, am I, where, I, ought to be judged. Unto the Jews, have I done no wrong, as, even thou, right well, art discovering.
text.KJV: 25:10 Then said Paul, I stand at Cesars iudgement seat, where I ought to bee iudged; to the Iewes haue I done no wrong, as thou very well knowest.
text.DRV: 25:10 And Paul said: At Cæsar's judgement seat do I stand, where I ought to be judged: the Jews I have not hurt, as thou very well knowest.
text.NABRE: 25:10 Paul answered, “I am standing before the tribunal of Caesar; this is where I should be tried. I have committed no crime against the Jews, as you very well know.

text.NRSV: Now if I am a wrongdoer and have committed anything for which I deserve to die, I am not trying to escape death; but if there is nothing to their charges against me, no one can turn me over to them. I appeal to the emperor.”
text.Rotherham: If then, on the one hand, I am doing wrong, and, anything worthy of death, have committed, I excuse not myself from dying; but, on the other hand, if there is, nothing, in the things whereof these are accusing me, no man, hath power to give, me, unto them as a favour:—Unto Caesar, I appeal!
text.KJV: 25:11 For if I be an offender, or haue committed any thing worthy of death, I refuse not to die: but if there be none of these things whereof these accuse me, no man may deliuer me vnto them. I appeale vnto Cesar.
text.DRV: 25:11 For if I have hurt them, or done any thing worthy of death, I refuse not to die. But if none of those things be, whereof these accuse me, no man can give me to them. I appeal to Cæsar.
text.NABRE: 25:11 If I have committed a crime or done anything deserving death, I do not seek to escape the death penalty; but if there is no substance to the charges they are bringing against me, then no one has the right to hand me over to them. I appeal to Caesar.”

text.NRSV: Then Festus, after he had conferred with his council, replied, “You have appealed to the emperor; to the emperor you shall go.”
text.Rotherham: Then Festus, having conversed with the council, answered—Unto Caesar, hast thou appealed? Unto Caesar, shalt thou go.
text.KJV: 25:12 Then Festus when he had conferred with the Councill, answered, Hast thou appealed vnto Cesar? vnto Cesar shalt thou goe.
text.DRV: 25:12 Then Festus having conferred with the Council, answered: Hast thou appealed to Cæsar? to Cæsar shalt thou go.
text.NABRE: 25:12 Then Festus, after conferring with his council, replied, “You have appealed to Caesar. To Caesar you will go.”

text.NRSV: After several days had passed, King Agrippa and Bernice arrived at Caesarea to welcome Festus.
text.Rotherham: And, some days having gone by, Agrippa the king and Bernice came down to Caesarea, to salute Festus.
text.KJV: 25:13 And after certaine dayes, king Agrippa and Bernice, came vnto Cesarea, to salute Festus.
text.DRV: 25:13 And when certain days were passed, king Agrippa & Bernice came down to Cæsarea to salute Festus.
text.NABRE: 25:13 Paul Before King Agrippa. When a few days had passed, King Agrippa and Bernice arrived in Caesarea on a visit to Festus.

text.NRSV: Since they were staying there several days, Festus laid Paul’s case before the king, saying, “There is a man here who was left in prison by Felix.
text.Rotherham: And, as they were spending more days there, Festus, repeated, unto the king, the things relating to Paul, saying—A certain man, hath been left behind by Felix, as a prisoner;
text.KJV: 25:14 And when they had beene there many dayes, Festus declared Pauls cause vnto the king, saying, There is a certaine man left in bonds by Felix:
text.DRV: 25:14 And as they tarried there a good many days, Festus signified to the king, of Paul, saying: A certain person was left prisoner by Felix,
text.NABRE: 25:14 Since they spent several days there, Festus referred Paul’s case to the king, saying, “There is a man here left in custody by Felix.

text.NRSV: When I was in Jerusalem, the chief priests and the elders of the Jews informed me about him and asked for a sentence against him.
text.Rotherham: concerning whom, when I happened to be in Jerusalem, the High-priests and the Elders of the Jews laid information, claiming against him a condemnation:
text.KJV: 25:15 About whom when I was at Hierusalem, the chiefe Priests and the Elders of the Iewes enformed me, desiring to haue iudgement against him.
text.DRV: 25:15 concerning whom, when I was at Jerusalem, the chief Priests and the Ancients of the Jews came unto me, desiring condemnation against him.
text.NABRE: 25:15 When I was in Jerusalem the chief priests and the elders of the Jews brought charges against him and demanded his condemnation.

text.NRSV: I replied that it was not the custom of the Romans to hand over anyone before the accused had met the accusers face to face and had been given an opportunity to make a defense against the charge.
text.Rotherham: unto whom made answer—That it is not a custom with Romans, to grant as a favour any man, before the accused, face to face, should have his accusers, and, opportunity of defence, should receive, concerning the charge.
text.KJV: 25:16 To whom I answered, It is not the maner of the Romanes to deliuer any man to die, before that he which is accused, haue the accusers face to face, and haue licence to answere for himselfe concerning the crime laid against him.
text.DRV: 25:16 To whom I answered: That it is not the Romans' custom to yield up any man before that he which is accused have his accusers present, and take place to make his answer for to clear himself of the crimes.
text.NABRE: 25:16 I answered them that it was not Roman practice to hand over an accused person before he has faced his accusers and had the opportunity to defend himself against their charge.

text.NRSV: So when they met here, I lost no time, but on the next day took my seat on the tribunal and ordered the man to be brought.
text.Rotherham: When, therefore, they had come together here, no delay whatever, making, on the next day, taking my place upon the judgment-seat, I ordered the man to be brought:
text.KJV: 25:17 Therefore when they were come hither, without any delay, on the morrow I sate on the iudgement seate, and cōmanded the man to be brought forth.
text.DRV: 25:17 When they therefore were assembled hither, without any delay, the day following, sitting in the judgement seat, I commanded the man to be brought.
text.NABRE: 25:17 So when [they] came together here, I made no delay; the next day I took my seat on the tribunal and ordered the man to be brought in.

text.NRSV: When the accusers stood up, they did not charge him with any of the crimes that I was expecting.
text.Rotherham: Concerning whom, taking their stand, his accusers, no accusation at all, were bringing, of the evil things which, I, had been suspecting;
text.KJV: 25:18 Against whom when the accusers stood vp, they brought none accusation of such things as I supposed:
text.DRV: 25:18 Of whom, when the accusers stood up, they brought no cause which I thought ill of:
text.NABRE: 25:18 His accusers stood around him, but did not charge him with any of the crimes I suspected.

text.NRSV: Instead they had certain points of disagreement with him about their own religion and about a certain Jesus, who had died, but whom Paul asserted to be alive.
text.Rotherham: but, certain questions concerning their own demon-worship, had they against him, and concerning one Jesus, who had died, whom Paul was affirming to be alive.
text.KJV: 25:19 But had certaine questions against him of their owne superstition, and of one Iesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be aliue.
text.DRV: 25:19 but certain questions of their own superstition they had against him, and of one Jesus deceased, whom Paul affirmed to live.
text.NABRE: 25:19 Instead they had some issues with him about their own religion and about a certain Jesus who had died but who Paul claimed was alive.

text.NRSV: Since I was at a loss how to investigate these questions, I asked whether he wished to go to Jerusalem and be tried there on these charges.
text.Rotherham: And, I, being at a loss as to the inquiry into these things, was asking—whether he might be minded to go unto Jerusalem, and, there, be judged concerning these things.
text.KJV: 25:20 And because I doubted of such maner of questions, I asked him whether he would goe to Hierusalem, and there be iudged of these matters.
text.DRV: 25:20 Doubting therefore of this kind of question, I said, whether he would go to Jerusalem, & there be judged of these things.
text.NABRE: 25:20 Since I was at a loss how to investigate this controversy, I asked if he were willing to go to Jerusalem and there stand trial on these charges.

text.NRSV: But when Paul had appealed to be kept in custody for the decision of his Imperial Majesty, I ordered him to be held until I could send him to the emperor.”
text.Rotherham: But, Paul, having appealed to be kept for the decision of the Emperor, I ordered him to be kept, until I could send him up unto Caesar.
text.KJV: 25:21 But when Paul had appealed to bee reserued vnto the hearing of Augustus, I commanded him to be kept, till I might send him to Cesar.
text.DRV: 25:21 But Paul appealing to be kept unto the knowledge of Augustus, I commanded him to be kept, till I send him to Cæsar.
text.NABRE: 25:21 And when Paul appealed that he be held in custody for the Emperor’s decision, I ordered him held until I could send him to Caesar.”

text.NRSV: Agrippa said to Festus, “I would like to hear the man myself.” “Tomorrow,” he said, “you will hear him.”
text.Rotherham: And, Agrippa, [said] unto Festus—I could wish, myself also, to hear, the man. To-morrow, (saith he) thou shalt hear him.
text.KJV: 25:22 Then Agrippa said vnto Festus, I would also heare the man my selfe. To morrow, said he, thou shalt heare him.
text.DRV: 25:22 And Agrippa said to Festus: Myself also would hear the man. Tomorrow, said he, thou shalt hear him.
text.NABRE: 25:22 Agrippa said to Festus, “I too should like to hear this man.” He replied, “Tomorrow you will hear him.”

text.NRSV: So on the next day Agrippa and Bernice came with great pomp, and they entered the audience hall with the military tribunes and the prominent men of the city. Then Festus gave the order and Paul was brought in.
text.Rotherham: On the morrow, therefore, when Agrippa had come, and Bernice, with great display, and they had entered into the audience-chamber, with the captains of thousands and men of distinction of the city,—and Festus had given orders, Paul was brought.
text.KJV: 25:23 And on the morrow when Agrippa was come and Bernice, with great pompe, and was entred into the place of hearing, with the chiefe captaines, and principall men of the citie; at Festus commaundement Paul was brought foorth.
text.DRV: 25:23 And the next day when Agrippa and Bernice were come with great pomp, and had entered into the hall of audience with the Tribunes and principal men of the city, at Festus' commandment Paul was brought.
text.NABRE: 25:23 The next day Agrippa and Bernice came with great ceremony and entered the audience hall in the company of cohort commanders and the prominent men of the city and, by command of Festus, Paul was brought in.

text.NRSV: And Festus said, “King Agrippa and all here present with us, you see this man about whom the whole Jewish community petitioned me, both in Jerusalem and here, shouting that he ought not to live any longer.
text.Rotherham: And Festus saith—King Agrippa! and all ye men, here present with us: Ye observe this person, concerning whom, one and all the throng of the Jews, have interceded with me, both in Jerusalem and here, crying aloud that he ought not to be living any longer.
text.KJV: 25:24 And Festus said, King Agrippa, and all men which are heere present with vs, ye see this man, about whom all the multitude of the Iewes haue dealt with me, both at Hierusalem, and also heere, crying that he ought not to liue any longer.
text.DRV: 25:24 And Festus saith: King Agrippa, and all ye men that are present together with us, you see this man, concerning whom all the multitude of the Jews called upon me at Jerusalem, requesting and crying out that he ought not to live any longer.
text.NABRE: 25:24 And Festus said, “King Agrippa and all you here present with us, look at this man about whom the whole Jewish populace petitioned me here and in Jerusalem, clamoring that he should live no longer.

text.NRSV: But I found that he had done nothing deserving death; and when he himself appealed to his Imperial Majesty, I decided to send him.
text.Rotherham: But, I, gathered, that, nothing worthy of death, had he committed; and, this man himself, having appealed unto the Emperor, I decided to send him:—
text.KJV: 25:25 But when I found that he had committed nothing worthy of death, and that he himselfe hath appealed to Augustus, I haue determined to send him.
text.DRV: 25:25 Yet have I found nothing that he hath committed worthy of death. But forasmuch as he himself appealed to Augustus, I have determined to send him.
text.NABRE: 25:25 I found, however, that he had done nothing deserving death, and so when he appealed to the Emperor, I decided to send him.

text.NRSV: But I have nothing definite to write to our sovereign about him. Therefore I have brought him before all of you, and especially before you, King Agrippa, so that, after we have examined him, I may have something to write—
text.Rotherham: Concerning whom, anything certain to write unto my lord, I have not; wherefore, I have brought him forth before you,—and especially before thee, King Agrippa! in order that, after examination had, I might have something I could write;
text.KJV: 25:26 Of whom I haue no certaine thing to write vnto my Lord: Wherefore I haue brought him foorth before you, and specially before thee, O king Agrippa, that after examination had, I might haue somewhat to write.
text.DRV: 25:26 Of whom what to write for certainty to my lord, I have not. For the which cause I have brought him forth to you, and especially to thee, king Agrippa, that examination being made, I may have what to write.
text.NABRE: 25:26 But I have nothing definite to write about him to our sovereign; therefore I have brought him before all of you, and particularly before you, King Agrippa, so that I may have something to write as a result of this investigation.

text.NRSV: for it seems to me unreasonable to send a prisoner without indicating the charges against him.”
text.Rotherham: For, unreasonable, unto me, it seemeth, when sending a prisoner, not also, the accusations against him, to signify.
text.KJV: 25:27 For it seemeth to me vnreasonable, to send a prisoner, and not withall to signifie the crimes laid against him.
text.DRV: 25:27 For it seemeth to me without reason, to send a prisoner, & not to signify his causes.
text.NABRE: 25:27 For it seems senseless to me to send up a prisoner without indicating the charges against him.”

text.NRSV: Agrippa said to Paul, “You have permission to speak for yourself.” Then Paul stretched out his hand and began his defense:
text.Rotherham: And, Agrippa, to Paul, said—It is permitted thee, on thine own behalf, to be speaking. Then Paul, stretching forth his hand, went on to make his defence.
text.KJV: 26:1 Then Agrippa said vnto Paul, Thou art permitted to speake for thy selfe. Then Paul stretched foorth the hand, and answered for himselfe,
text.DRV: 26:1 But Agrippa said to Paul: Thou art permitted to speak for thyself. Then Paul stretching forth his hand, began to make his answer:
text.NABRE: 26:1 Chapter 26 - King Agrippa Hears Paul. Then Agrippa said to Paul, “You may now speak on your own behalf.” So Paul stretched out his hand and began his defense.

text.NRSV: “I consider myself fortunate that it is before you, King Agrippa, I am to make my defense today against all the accusations of the Jews,
text.Rotherham: Concerning all things of which I am accused by Jews, King Agrippa, I have been counting myself happy, that, before thee, am I about, this day, to be making my defence;
text.KJV: 26:2 I thinke my selfe happy, king Agrippa, because I shall answere for my selfe this day before thee touching all the things whereof I am accused of the Iewes:
text.DRV: 26:2 Touching all things whereof I am accused of the Jews, king Agrippa, I account myself happy for that I am to defend myself this day before thee,
text.NABRE: 26:2 “I count myself fortunate, King Agrippa, that I am to defend myself before you today against all the charges made against me by the Jews,

text.NRSV: especially because you are familiar with all the customs and controversies of the Jews; therefore I beg you to listen to me patiently.
text.Rotherham: especially, as thou art, well-versed, in all the Jewish customs and questions. Wherefore, I beseech thee, patiently, to hear me.
text.KJV: 26:3 Especially, because I know thee to be expert in all customes and questions which are among the Iewes: wherefore I beseech thee to heare mee patiently.
text.DRV: 26:3 especially whereas thou knowest all things that are among the Jews, customs and questions: for the which cause I beseech thee, hear me patiently.
text.NABRE: 26:3 especially since you are an expert in all the Jewish customs and controversies. And therefore I beg you to listen patiently.

text.NRSV: “All the Jews know my way of life from my youth, a life spent from the beginning among my own people and in Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: My manner of life, then, from my youth, which, from its commencement, was formed among my nation, even in Jerusalem, know all Jews,
text.KJV: 26:4 My maner of life from my youth, which was at the first among mine owne nation at Hierusalem, know all the Iewes,
text.DRV: 26:4 And my life truly from my youth, which was from the beginning in my nation in Jerusalem, all the Jews do know:
text.NABRE: 26:4 My manner of living from my youth, a life spent from the beginning among my people and in Jerusalem, all [the] Jews know.

text.NRSV: They have known for a long time, if they are willing to testify, that I have lived as a Pharisee according to the strictest sect of our religion.
text.Rotherham: inasmuch as they were aforetime observing me, from the outset,—if they please to bear witness,—that, according to the strictest sect of our own religion, I lived, a Pharisee.
text.KJV: 26:5 Which knew me from the beginning, (if they would testifie) that after the most straitest sect of our religion, I liued a Pharisee.
text.DRV: 26:5 knowing me before from the beginning (if they will give testimony) that according to the most sure sect of our religion I lived a Pharisee.
text.NABRE: 26:5 They have known about me from the start, if they are willing to testify, that I have lived my life as a Pharisee, the strictest party of our religion.

text.NRSV: And now I stand here on trial on account of my hope in the promise made by God to our ancestors,
text.Rotherham: And, now, for the hope of the promise, unto our fathers, being brought to pass by God, am I standing to be judged,—
text.KJV: 26:6 And now I stand, and am iudged for the hope of the promise made of God vnto our fathers:
text.DRV: 26:6 And now for the hope of the promise that was made of God to our fathers, do I stand subject to judgement:
text.NABRE: 26:6 But now I am standing trial because of my hope in the promise made by God to our ancestors.

text.NRSV: a promise that our twelve tribes hope to attain, as they earnestly worship day and night. It is for this hope, your Majesty, that I am accused by Jews!
text.Rotherham: unto which [hope], our twelve-tribed nation, with intensity, night and day, rendering divine service, is hoping to attain—concerning which hope, I am being accused by Jews, O King!
text.KJV: 26:7 Vnto which promise our twelue tribes instantly seruing God day and night, hope to come: For which hopes sake, King Agrippa, I am accused of the Iewes.
text.DRV: 26:7 the which, our twelve tribes serving night and day, hope to come unto. Of the which hope, o king, I am accused of the Jews.
text.NABRE: 26:7 Our twelve tribes hope to attain to that promise as they fervently worship God day and night; and on account of this hope I am accused by Jews, O king.

text.NRSV: Why is it thought incredible by any of you that God raises the dead?
text.Rotherham: What! incredible, is it judged with you, that, God the dead doth raise?
text.KJV: 26:8 Why should it be thought a thing incredible with you, that God should raise the dead?
text.DRV: 26:8 What incredible thing is it judged with you, if God raise the dead?
text.NABRE: 26:8 Why is it thought unbelievable among you that God raises the dead?

text.NRSV: “Indeed, I myself was convinced that I ought to do many things against the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
text.Rotherham: I, therefore, imagined to myself, that, against the name of Jesus the Nazarene, it was needful, many hostile things, to bring about,—
text.KJV: 26:9 I verily thought with my selfe, that I ought to doe many things contrary to the name of Iesus of Nazareth:
text.DRV: 26:9 And myself truly had thought that I ought to do against the name of Jesus of Nazareth many contrary things.
text.NABRE: 26:9 I myself once thought that I had to do many things against the name of Jesus the Nazorean,

text.NRSV: And that is what I did in Jerusalem; with authority received from the chief priests, I not only locked up many of the saints in prison, but I also cast my vote against them when they were condemned to death.
text.Rotherham: which also I did in Jerusalem, yea and, many of the saints, I myself, in prisons, shut up,—the authority, from the High-priests, having received: and, when they were to be put to death, I brought against them my vote;
text.KJV: 26:10 Which thing I also did in Hierusalem, and many of the Saints did I shut vp in prison, hauing receiued authoritie from the chiefe Priests, and when they were put to death, I gaue my voyce against them.
text.DRV: 26:10 Which also I did at Jerusalem, and many of the saints did I shut up in prisons, having received authority of the chief priests: and when they were put to death, I brought the sentence.
text.NABRE: 26:10 and I did so in Jerusalem. I imprisoned many of the holy ones with the authorization I received from the chief priests, and when they were to be put to death I cast my vote against them.

text.NRSV: By punishing them often in all the synagogues I tried to force them to blaspheme; and since I was so furiously enraged at them, I pursued them even to foreign cities.
text.Rotherham: and, throughout all the synagogues, ofttimes punishing them, I would fain have compelled them to defame; and, being excessively maddened against them, I went on to pursue them as far as even the outlying cities.
text.KJV: 26:11 And I punished them oft in euery Synagogue, and compelled them to blaspheme, and being exceedingly mad against them, I persecuted them euen vnto strange cities.
text.DRV: 26:11 And throughout all the synagogues oftentimes punishing them, I compelled them to blaspheme: and yet more mad against them, I persecuted them even unto foreign cities.
text.NABRE: 26:11 Many times, in synagogue after synagogue, I punished them in an attempt to force them to blaspheme; I was so enraged against them that I pursued them even to foreign cities.

text.NRSV: “With this in mind I was traveling to Damascus with the authority and commission of the chief priests,
text.Rotherham: Among which things, being on a journey unto Damascus, with the authority and commission of the High-priests,
text.KJV: 26:12 Whereupon, as I went to Damascus, with authoritie and commission from the chiefe Priests:
text.DRV: 26:12 Among which things whiles I went to Damascus with authority and permission of the chief priests,
text.NABRE: 26:12 “On one such occasion I was traveling to Damascus with the authorization and commission of the chief priests.

text.NRSV: when at midday along the road, your Majesty, I saw a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, shining around me and my companions.
text.Rotherham: at mid-day, on the road, I saw, O King, from heaven, above the splendour of the sun, shining around me, a light, and [around] them who, with me, were journeying;
text.KJV: 26:13 At midday, O king, I saw in the way a light from heauen, aboue the brightnes of the Sunne, shining round about mee, and them which iourneyed with me.
text.DRV: 26:13 at mid-day, in the way, I saw (o king) from Heaven a light to have shined round about me and them that were in company with me, above the brightness of the sun.
text.NABRE: 26:13 At midday, along the way, O king, I saw a light from the sky, brighter than the sun, shining around me and my traveling companions.

text.NRSV: When we had all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice saying to me in the Hebrew language, ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It hurts you to kick against the goads.’
text.Rotherham: and, when we were all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice, saying unto me, in the Hebrew language—Saul! Saul! why, me, art thou persecuting? It is hard for thee, against goads, to be kicking!
text.KJV: 26:14 And when wee were all fallen to the earth, I heard a voice speaking vnto me, and saying in the Hebrew tongue, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me? It is hard for thee to kicke against the prickes.
text.DRV: 26:14 And when all we were fallen down on the ground, I heard a voice speaking to me in the Hebrew tongue: Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me? It is hard for thee to kick against the prick.
text.NABRE: 26:14 We all fell to the ground and I heard a voice saying to me in Hebrew, ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It is hard for you to kick against the goad.’

text.NRSV: I asked, ‘Who are you, Lord?’ And the Lord answered, ‘I am Jesus whom you are persecuting.
text.Rotherham: And, I, said—Who art thou, Lord? And the Lord said—I, am Jesus, whom, thou, art persecuting!
text.KJV: 26:15 And I said, Who art thou, Lord? And hee said, I am Iesus whom thou persecutest.
text.DRV: 26:15 And I said: Who are thou Lord? And our Lord answered: I am Jesus whom thou doest persecute.
text.NABRE: 26:15 And I said, ‘Who are you, sir?’ And the Lord replied, ‘I am Jesus whom you are persecuting.

text.NRSV: But get up and stand on your feet; for I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you to serve and testify to the things in which you have seen me and to those in which I will appear to you.
text.Rotherham: But rise and stand upon thy feet; for, to this end, have I appeared unto thee,—To appoint thee an attendant and a witness, both of the things as to which thou hast seen me, and of those as to which I will appear unto thee:
text.KJV: 26:16 But rise, and stand vpon thy feete, for I haue appeared vnto thee for this purpose, to make thee a minister and a witnesse, both of these things which thou hast seene, & of those things in the which I will appeare vnto thee,
text.DRV: 26:16 But rise up and stand upon thy feet: for to this end have I appeared to thee, that I may ordain thee a minister and witness of those things which thou hast seen, and of those things wherein I will appear to thee,
text.NABRE: 26:16 Get up now, and stand on your feet. I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you as a servant and witness of what you have seen [of me] and what you will be shown.

text.NRSV: I will rescue you from your people and from the Gentiles—to whom I am sending you
text.Rotherham: Rescuing thee from among the people, and from among the nations, unto whom, I, am sending thee—
text.KJV: 26:17 Deliuering thee from the people, and from the Gentiles, vnto whom now I send thee,
text.DRV: 26:17 delivering thee out of the peoples and nations unto the which now I send thee,
text.NABRE: 26:17 I shall deliver you from this people and from the Gentiles to whom I send you,

text.NRSV: to open their eyes so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, so that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are sanctified by faith in me.’
text.Rotherham: To open their eyes; that they turn from darkness unto light, and the authority of Satan unto God, that they may receive remission of sins, and an inheritance among them who have been made holy by the faith respecting me.
text.KJV: 26:18 To open their eyes, and to turne them from darknesse to light, and from the power of Satan vnto God, that they may receiue forgiuenesse of sinnes, and inheritance among them which are sanctified by faith that is in me.
text.DRV: 26:18 to open their eyes, that they may be converted from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive remission of sins, and lot among the saints by the faith that is in me.
text.NABRE: 26:18 to open their eyes that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, so that they may obtain forgiveness of sins and an inheritance among those who have been consecrated by faith in me.’

text.NRSV: “After that, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision,
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, O King Agrippa,—I became not disobedient unto the heavenly vision;
text.KJV: 26:19 Whereupon, O king Agrippa, I was not disobedient vnto the heauenly vision:
text.DRV: 26:19 Whereupon, king Agrippa, I was not incredulous to the heavenly vision:
text.NABRE: 26:19 “And so, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision.

text.NRSV: but declared first to those in Damascus, then in Jerusalem and throughout the countryside of Judea, and also to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God and do deeds consistent with repentance.
text.Rotherham: But—both to them in Damascus, first, and in Jerusalem, unto all the country of Judaea also, and unto the nations, I carried tidings—that they should repent, and turn unto God, and, works worthy of their repentance, should practise.
text.KJV: 26:20 But shewed first vnto them of Damascus, and at Hierusalem, and thorowout all the coasts of Iudea, and then to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turne to God, and do works meete for repentance.
text.DRV: 26:20 but to them first that are at Damascus, and at Jerusalem, and unto all the country of Jewry, and to the Gentiles did I preach that they should do penance, & turn to God, doing works worthy of penance.
text.NABRE: 26:20 On the contrary, first to those in Damascus and in Jerusalem and throughout the whole country of Judea, and then to the Gentiles, I preached the need to repent and turn to God, and to do works giving evidence of repentance.

text.NRSV: For this reason the Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me.
